text: The surgeon observes vasodilation of the cystic duct and common bile duct (CBD) and proceeds to immobilize the duodenum from the cystic duct. The peritoneum covering the gallbladder is carefully opened, ensuring dissection remains above the level of Rouvière's sulcus.

The surgeon uses a hook to dissect fiber by fiber, skeletonizing the cystic duct within the Calot's triangle. Low current monopolar cautery is applied anteriorly near the cystic gland, with attention to the node serving as a landmark for the cystic artery.
The surgeon separates the peritoneum covering the node and opens the peritoneum over the catheter, proceeding to dissect towards the cystic duct to skeletonize it.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to further dissect the cystic duct and artery within the clearly exposed Calot's triangle. The cystic artery is isolated, and the common bile duct (CBD) is visualized. The duodenum is separated from the CBD and cystic duct. Clips are applied: three on the cystic duct and one on the cystic artery.

The surgeon coils and cuts the cystic artery above the applied clip. After ligating the cystic artery, the pulsation becomes visible. The gallbladder is then separated from the liver bed using a monopolar hook, with the peritoneum being stripped from the gallbladder.

The gallbladder is fully released from the liver bed and is suspended from the cystic duct. The gallbladder is separated from the cystic duct, ensuring that only one lumen is attached during the cutting process.
